text:
INPATIENT HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY ADMISSION NOTE
Acute Myeloid Leukemia – Induction Chemotherapy Admission

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Marcus T. Delgado is a 48-year-old male high school science teacher admitted to the inpatient hematology/oncology service for initiation of induction chemotherapy for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Delgado is a previously healthy 48-year-old non-smoking male who presented to his primary care physician approximately three weeks prior to this admission with progressive fatigue, generalized pallor, easy bruising, and notable gingival swelling with spontaneous bleeding of the gums. He also reported intermittent epistaxis over the preceding 10 days. He denied fevers, night sweats, or weight loss at that time. A complete blood count obtained in the outpatient setting was markedly abnormal, with a white blood cell count of 84,000/uL, hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL, and platelets of 28,000/uL. He was referred emergently to our institution for urgent hematologic evaluation.

Upon presentation to our leukemia clinic, peripheral blood smear demonstrated 62% circulating blasts. He was admitted to the inpatient service for expedited workup. Bilateral posterior iliac crest bone marrow biopsy and aspirate confirmed AML with 78% blasts by morphology. Immunophenotyping by flow cytometry demonstrated a CD34+/CD117+/CD13+/CD33+/HLA-DR+/MPO+ immunophenotype, consistent with AML without maturation (FAB M1). Conventional cytogenetics (G-band karyotype) showed a normal male karyotype, 46,XY. Comprehensive myeloid NGS panel (283-gene) identified a FLT3 internal tandem duplication (ITD) at the exon 14-15 junction with an allelic ratio of 0.87 (high; VAF 47%), as well as a co-occurring DNMT3A p.R882H mutation (VAF 42%). NPM1 was wild-type. No IDH1/2, ASXL1, TP53, or RUNX1 mutations were identified. A PTPN11 p.E76K variant was detected at low VAF (18%), likely representing a leukemic subclone. No cytogenetically cryptic fusions (RUNX1-RUNX1T1, CBFB-MYH11, PML-RARA, BCR-ABL1, KMT2A rearrangement) were detected. Per ELN 2022 criteria, this disease is classified as adverse risk, primarily driven by the FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in the setting of NPM1 wild-type.

Stagging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast demonstrated mild hepatomegaly (liver span 17.2 cm) and mild splenomegaly (spleen 14.1 cm), consistent with systemic disease involvement. No discrete lymphadenopathy. No chloroma or extramedullary disease was identified. Marrow signal was diffusely abnormal on bony windows, consistent with leukemic infiltration. LDH was elevated at 1,240 U/L.

Lumbar puncture was performed prior to this admission and CSF cytology was negative for blasts, confirming no CNS involvement. Echocardiogram showed preserved left ventricular function with EF of 62% and no wall motion abnormalities, confirming suitability for anthracycline-based therapy.

HLA typing has been submitted for unrelated donor search through NMDP in anticipation of allogeneic stem cell transplant in first remission, given adverse-risk disease.

Mr. Delgado is now admitted for initiation of induction chemotherapy with 7+3 (cytarabine/daunorubicin) plus midostaurin per the RATIFY trial regimen.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Hypertension – well controlled, currently managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily
2. No prior hematologic conditions
3. No prior malignancy
4. No prior chemotherapy or radiation

---

SURGICAL HISTORY
No prior surgeries.

---

FAMILY HISTORY
Maternal uncle with colon cancer, diagnosed at approximately age 62. No other known family history of hematologic malignancy, leukemia, or lymphoma. No known familial cancer syndromes.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY
Mr. Delgado works as a high school science teacher. He is married with two teenage children. He is a lifetime non-smoker. He drinks alcohol only occasionally (1-2 drinks per month). He does not use recreational drugs or tobacco products. He lives in a single-family home. He has good social support from family. He is insured. He denies any significant occupational chemical or radiation exposures.

---

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies. No known food allergies.

---

MEDICATIONS (Prior to Admission)
1. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Occasional ibuprofen as needed (HELD on admission due to thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk)

Medications Initiated on Admission (per Leukemia Induction Protocol):
1. Allopurinol 300 mg orally once daily (tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis)
2. IV hydration (aggressive; sodium bicarbonate added to first liter per TLS protocol)
3. Fluconazole 400 mg orally once daily (antifungal prophylaxis)
4. Levofloxacin 500 mg orally once daily (antibacterial prophylaxis)
5. Valacyclovir 500 mg orally twice daily (antiviral prophylaxis)
6. Ondansetron 8 mg IV every 8 hours scheduled (antiemetic)
7. Dexamethasone 8 mg IV once daily (antiemetic adjunct per protocol, short course)
8. Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily (GI prophylaxis)

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Reports significant fatigue and malaise over the past three weeks, now gradually worsening. No fevers on admission today (has had low-grade temperatures at home on isolated occasions). Denies significant night sweats. No unintentional weight loss reported (though appetite has been mildly decreased).
HEENT: Gingival swelling and spontaneous bleeding of the gums ongoing, somewhat improved since being placed on local measures. Intermittent epistaxis with the most recent episode five days ago. No vision changes. No ear pain or discharge.
Cardiovascular: Occasional mild palpitations, attributed to anemia. No chest pain. No dyspnea on exertion beyond baseline fatigue.
Respiratory: No cough, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis. No pleuritic chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea over the past week. No vomiting. Decreased appetite. No hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. No abdominal pain.
Genitourinary: No gross hematuria. No dysuria or urinary frequency changes beyond baseline.
Musculoskeletal: Mild diffuse bone aching, predominantly in the bilateral lower extremities; present for approximately two weeks. No joint swelling.
Skin/Lymph: Easy bruising, noted on bilateral arms and thighs. No petechiae reported by patient.
Neurological: No headaches, visual changes, or neurologic deficits. No focal weakness or sensory changes. No confusion.
Psychosocial: Patient reports significant anxiety regarding the diagnosis and its implications for his family and career. He is engaged and asking detailed questions. His wife is present and also anxious.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Temperature 37.1 C (98.8 F), blood pressure 138/84 mmHg, heart rate 102 bpm (mildly tachycardic, consistent with anemia), respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Height 5'11" (180 cm), weight 78 kg, BMI 24.1, BSA 1.95 m2.

General: Alert and oriented, mild pallor visible, mildly anxious but cooperative. No acute respiratory distress.

HEENT: Mild conjunctival pallor bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, and reactive. Oral mucosa: gingival hypertrophy most prominent along the anterior maxillary and mandibular ridges, with hyperemic, friable gingival tissue and trace ooze noted at the gumline. No frank active hemorrhage at time of exam. No petechiae on the palate. No thrush. Tongue is normal. Oropharynx without exudate. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Neck: Supple. No lymphadenopathy. No thyromegaly. JVP not elevated.

Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Normal work of breathing.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Mild S1/S2, no murmurs appreciated. No S3 or S4. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended in the upper abdomen. Mild discomfort on deep palpation of the left upper quadrant. Spleen palpable approximately 3 cm below the left costal margin; smooth, non-tender. Liver is palpable at the right costal margin with a span estimated at approximately 16-17 cm by percussion, consistent with mild hepatomegaly. No ascites. Bowel sounds normoactive.

Extremities: Multiple ecchymoses of varying age over bilateral forearms, upper arms, and thighs. No petechiae on lower extremities at time of exam. No active bleeding at PICC insertion site. No calf tenderness.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all tested muscle groups. Sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. No cerebellar dysmetria. Gait not formally assessed given thrombocytopenia and fall risk precautions.

Skin: Pallor. Scattered ecchymoses as noted. No skin infiltration, nodules, or chloromatous skin lesions. No rash.

Lymph: No axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Back: No sternal tenderness. Mild diffuse lower thoracic and lumbar vertebral tenderness on palpation, likely reflecting marrow expansion.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 84,000/uL (markedly elevated); differential: 62% blasts, 18% neutrophils, 12% lymphocytes, 4% monocytes, 4% other
  - Hemoglobin: 7.2 g/dL (microcytic-normocytic; low)
  - Hematocrit: 22%
  - MCV: 87 fL
  - Platelets: 28,000/uL (severely thrombocytopenic)
  - ANC: ~15,100/uL (largely blast-driven; functional neutropenia)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  - Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
  - Chloride: 101 mEq/L
  - Bicarbonate: 23 mEq/L
  - BUN: 18 mg/dL
  - Creatinine: 0.85 mg/dL (GFR >80 mL/min)
  - Glucose: 98 mg/dL
  - AST: 38 U/L
  - ALT: 32 U/L
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 87 U/L
  - Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL
  - Albumin: 3.6 g/dL
  - Total protein: 6.8 g/dL

Additional Labs:
  - LDH: 1,240 U/L (markedly elevated, consistent with high disease burden)
  - Uric acid: 8.7 mg/dL (elevated; TLS risk)
  - Phosphorus: 5.1 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
  - Calcium: 8.8 mg/dL
  - Magnesium: 2.0 mEq/L
  - INR: 1.3 (mildly elevated)
  - PT: 15.2 seconds
  - PTT: 33 seconds
  - Fibrinogen: 210 mg/dL
  - Peripheral blood blast %: 62% (confirms markedly elevated circulating blast burden)

Virology/Infectious Screening:
  - CMV IgG: positive (seropositive)
  - EBV IgG: positive
  - HSV IgG: positive
  - VZV IgG: positive
  - HIV-1/2 antigen/antibody combo: negative
  - Hepatitis B surface antigen: negative; core antibody: negative; surface antibody: negative
  - Hepatitis C antibody: negative

Cardiac:
  - Echocardiogram (transthoracic): Left ventricular ejection fraction 62%, normal wall motion, no pericardial effusion, no valvular pathology. Suitable for anthracycline-based therapy.

CSF Studies (lumbar puncture performed prior to admission):
  - Opening pressure: 12 cm H2O
  - WBC: 1 cell/uL (normal)
  - RBC: 1 cell/uL
  - Protein: 38 mg/dL
  - Glucose: 62 mg/dL
  - Cytology: NEGATIVE for malignant cells/blasts
  - No CNS involvement confirmed

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast (obtained within the past week as part of pre-treatment workup):
Mild hepatomegaly with a liver span of 17.2 cm craniocaudally, without focal hepatic lesions or biliary dilatation. Mild splenomegaly with the spleen measuring 14.1 cm in its longest axis, homogeneous enhancement, no focal splenic lesion. No mediastinal, hilar, axillary, or retroperitoneal adenopathy. Lungs clear; no consolidation, nodule, or pleural effusion. Kidneys: symmetric bilateral enhancement; no masses. No pelvic adenopathy. Osseous structures: diffuse marrow signal abnormality compatible with marrow infiltration. No lytic or blastic lesions. Notably, no chloroma (granulocytic sarcoma) identified. Overall findings consistent with systemic leukemic involvement limited to bone marrow and hepatosplenomegaly; no discrete extramedullary AML deposits.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. De novo Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), FAB M1 (AML without maturation), WHO 2022 classification: AML, NOS
   - Presenting WBC 84,000/uL, 62% peripheral blasts, 78% marrow blasts
   - Normal karyotype, 46,XY
   - FLT3-ITD exon 14-15, allelic ratio 0.87 (high) — ELN adverse risk determinant
   - DNMT3A R882H co-mutation (VAF 42%) — known leukemogenic co-driver, associated with clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) ancestry and adverse prognosis
   - NPM1 wild-type; IDH1/IDH2 wild-type; TP53 wild-type
   - ELN 2022 risk: ADVERSE (FLT3-ITD high AR, NPM1 wild-type)
   - No CNS involvement
   - No extramedullary disease by CT
   - ECOG performance status 1

2. Symptomatic anemia (Hgb 7.2 g/dL) — transfusion support being provided

3. Severe thrombocytopenia (platelets 28,000/uL) with active gingival bleeding — platelet transfusion support to maintain counts >20,000/uL (>50,000 if active bleeding)

4. Hypertension — well controlled on lisinopril; continue home antihypertensive

5. Anxiety regarding diagnosis — counseling support offered; social work referral placed

---

PLAN

1. AML Induction Chemotherapy – 7+3 Plus Midostaurin (RATIFY Regimen):
   - Cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day as continuous IV infusion over 24 hours, days 1 through 7 (total 7 days). BSA 1.95 m2; actual dose approximately 195 mg/day (rounded per institutional protocol to 200 mg/day). Administered via PICC line.
   - Daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV bolus over 30-60 minutes on days 1, 2, and 3. Actual dose approximately 117 mg/day (rounded per protocol). Total cumulative anthracycline: ~350 mg/m2.
   - Midostaurin (Rydapt) 50 mg orally twice daily on days 8 through 21. To be taken with a meal to minimize GI side effects. Rationale: FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio; RATIFY trial demonstrated OS benefit of midostaurin added to 7+3 induction in FLT3-mutated AML.
   - PICC line (double lumen) confirmed patent and in appropriate position by chest X-ray prior to initiating chemotherapy.

2. Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS) Prophylaxis and Management:
   - High risk for TLS given leukocytosis (WBC 84,000), markedly elevated LDH (1,240), hyperuricemia (uric acid 8.7), and hyperphosphatemia (phosphorus 5.1).
   - Allopurinol 300 mg orally daily (xanthine oxidase inhibitor; adequate renal function confirmed).
   - IV hydration: 2-3 L/day; monitoring for fluid overload and dilutional complications.
   - Electrolytes (potassium, phosphorus, calcium, uric acid, creatinine) to be monitored every 6-8 hours for the first 72 hours of chemotherapy, then daily.
   - Rasburicase available as rescue for severe hyperuricemia if allopurinol insufficient.
   - Urine output target >100 mL/hour.

3. Transfusion Support:
   - Packed red blood cells (pRBC): transfuse to maintain Hgb ≥7-8 g/dL; pre-medicate with diphenhydramine and acetaminophen given expected multiple transfusions.
   - Platelets: single-donor apheresis platelets to maintain counts ≥20,000/uL; increase threshold to ≥50,000/uL in setting of active bleeding (current gingival bleeding).
   - All blood products: leukoreduced and irradiated (anticipated transplant candidate; CMV-safe per institutional protocol).

4. Antimicrobial Prophylaxis:
   - Fluconazole 400 mg orally daily (antifungal; Candida prophylaxis during anticipated neutropenia)
   - Levofloxacin 500 mg orally daily (antibacterial; gram-negative coverage during neutropenia)
   - Valacyclovir 500 mg orally twice daily (HSV prophylaxis)
   - Patient counseled on febrile neutropenia precautions: neutropenic diet, hand hygiene, fever threshold (>38.3 C or >38.0 C sustained).

5. Antiemetic Therapy:
   - Ondansetron 8 mg IV every 8 hours scheduled throughout chemotherapy days
   - Dexamethasone 8 mg IV daily for 3 days as adjunct antiemetic (days 1-3 of daunorubicin)
   - Lorazepam 0.5 mg IV/PO as needed for breakthrough nausea or anxiety
   - Prochlorperazine available as second rescue antiemetic

6. Gingival Bleeding Management:
   - Oral rinses with tranexamic acid mouthwash (if available per pharmacy) and chlorhexidine rinse
   - Platelet transfusion to maintain counts ≥50,000/uL until active bleeding resolves
   - Dental surgery consultation deferred; conservative management only during induction
   - Soft diet; avoid hard or crunchy foods

7. HLA Typing and Transplant Planning:
   - HLA typing (10-locus: HLA-A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1) submitted prior to this admission
   - Unrelated donor search to be initiated through NMDP immediately upon submission
   - Given ELN adverse-risk disease (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 wild-type, DNMT3A co-mutation), allogeneic stem cell transplant in first complete remission (CR1) is strongly indicated per current guidelines and our institutional recommendation
   - Patient and family educated on transplant pathway, expected timeline, and rationale

8. Monitoring and Safety:
   - Daily CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, LFTs, and coagulation studies during induction
   - Daily weights and fluid balance monitoring
   - Echocardiographic baseline established; cardiotoxicity risk counseling provided
   - Serial ECGs not routinely required but available if QTc concerns arise with antiemetics
   - Cardiac: lisinopril held during induction (hemodynamic and electrolyte monitoring concerns); blood pressure monitoring daily
   - Isolation precautions: neutropenic precautions to be implemented once ANC declines
   - Physical therapy consultation placed for mobility and deconditioning prevention

9. CNS Prophylaxis:
   - CNS involvement excluded by negative CSF cytology
   - Routine intrathecal prophylaxis not indicated for de novo AML without CNS disease in this clinical context
   - Will reassess if CNS symptoms develop

10. Psychosocial and Patient Education:
    - Patient and wife extensively counseled regarding diagnosis, treatment plan, expected course of induction (anticipated 4-6 weeks of hospital admission), expected side effects including prolonged neutropenia (nadir expected days 10-18), febrile neutropenia, mucositis, alopecia, nausea/vomiting, fatigue, and transfusion dependency
    - Social work referral placed for psychological support and resource navigation
    - Financial counseling referral placed (patient is a teacher; employment leave expected)
    - Leave of absence paperwork discussed; occupational therapy referral pending
    - Written educational materials on AML and induction chemotherapy provided
    - Midostaurin-specific counseling provided (GI side effects, drug-drug interaction awareness, medication timing with meals)

Day 1 of 7+3 induction initiated today. Patient tolerated first dose of daunorubicin and cytarabine infusion without immediate adverse reaction. Midostaurin to begin on day 8 after completion of cytarabine infusion.

Inpatient attending: Dr. [name redacted], Hematology/Oncology
Note author: Hematology/Oncology Fellow
Co-signed by attending oncologist.
